_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Meigs County, OH. County formed 1819 from Gallia and Athens counties. Ohio River county subject to flooding — the 1937 flood caused record damage at Pomeroy.

previous_courthouses.fate: County seat was at Chester until moved to Pomeroy in 1841. Early records transferred.
Frame and brick courthouses on the river bottomland. Replaced by the 1877 building.

records_destroyed: some loose papers in basement storage
records_survived: deed books (bound volumes on upper floors)
record_losses.description: The 1937 Ohio River flood inundated Pomeroy, including the courthouse basement and ground floor. Bound record volumes stored on upper floors survived, but loose papers, some vital records, and documents in basement storage suffered water damage. Some records were permanently lost; others were damaged but partially legible. The flood is the primary record loss event for Meigs County. Later floods caused minor damage, but the 1937 event was catastrophic.
record_losses.narrative_impact: The 1937 flood did not destroy Meigs County's core records — deed books and will books survived on upper floors — but loose papers and some vital records stored in the basement were damaged or lost. If {ancestor_name}'s records have gaps in the early twentieth century, the 1937 flood is a likely explanation.
record_losses.source: Ohio Historical Society. Guide to County Records. Ohio Emergency Management Agency, 1937 flood records.

deeds.completeness: Good — bound volumes survived the 1937 flood. Continuous from formation.
deeds.gaps: Some early loose instruments may have been lost in 1937 flood damage.
deeds.repository: Meigs County Recorder's Office; Ohio History Connection microfilm

wills_estates.gaps: Loose estate papers from the earliest decades are fragmentary. Some 1937 flood damage to papers.
wills_estates.repository: Meigs County Probate Court; Ohio History Connection

marriages.gaps: Minor gaps. Some early marriage returns may have been damaged.
marriages.repository: Meigs County Probate Court; Ohio History Connection microfilm

court_minutes.completeness: Good — Court of Common Pleas records survive from formation
court_minutes.gaps: Minor water damage to some volumes.
court_minutes.repository: Meigs County Clerk of Courts; Ohio History Connection

vital_records.completeness: Uneven compliance until early 1900s. Some pre-1937 vital records damaged in the flood.
vital_records.repository: Meigs County Health Department; Ohio Department of Health

substitute_sources.description: Meigs County is enumerated from 1820. Agricultural and manufacturing schedules available.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for bridging gaps caused by the 1937 flood damage. Census data provides baseline family information when county records are damaged.

substitute_sources.description: Methodist and Baptist churches dominated Meigs County's religious landscape. Church records supplement civil records for births, marriages, and deaths.
substitute_sources.repository: Local congregations; Ohio History Connection; United Methodist Archives
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Particularly important for pre-1867 vital events and for periods where county records were damaged.

substitute_sources.source_type: Newspaper records — Meigs County Pioneer / Pomeroy newspapers
substitute_sources.description: Local newspapers published death notices, marriage announcements, estate notices, and legal advertisements that duplicate or supplement courthouse records.
substitute_sources.repository: Meigs County Library; Ohio History Connection microfilm
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Valuable for reconstructing events documented in court records that may have been damaged in 1937. Newspaper death notices and estate advertisements are independent records.

narrative_hooks: The 1937 flood put the Meigs County courthouse under water along with the rest of Pomeroy — the bound record books on the upper floors survived, but the loose papers in the basement did not. If {ancestor_name}'s early vital records are missing, the river took them.

Pomeroy was built on the narrowest bottomland of any county seat in Ohio — the courthouse sat between the river and the hills with nowhere to go when the water rose. {ancestor_name}'s family records survived the flood if they were in bound volumes; they did not if they were in the basement.

Before the 1937 flood, Meigs County's records ran continuously from 1819. After the flood, some gaps appeared — not in the deed books or the will books, which were saved, but in the loose papers that documented the everyday transactions of families like {ancestor_name}'s.

Ohio required vital registration from 1867, but the 1937 flood damaged some early records — if {ancestor_name}'s birth or death record from the pre-1937 period is missing, check church records and newspaper notices as substitutes.
source_references: Ohio Historical Society. Guide to County Records in the Ohio Historical Society Archives.
Ohio Emergency Management Agency. 1937 Ohio River Flood Records.
Larkin, Stillman Carter. The Pioneer History of Meigs County. Columbus: Berlin Printing, 1908.
